story:
I'm not your average pine. I've rooted here since before Elmswick was a dot on a surveyor's map, and while bark doesn't make for a good mouth, my thoughts carry just fine to anyone who knows how to listen. Last Christmas Eve, those thoughts solved a mystery that almost spoiled the holiday for every creature in the woods and every kid in town.

It started with a theft: all of Elmswick's Christmas lights vanished on December 23. The star on Town Hall, the twinkling porch strands, even the gaudy glowing reindeer—gone. Humans blamed vandals, gremlins, and Gary's kids. My roots, which hear what the soil would rather keep to itself, told me something stranger had crept into the forest.

Just before dawn, tiny boots crunched through the snow. Enter one elf, ears sharp enough to spear chestnuts, hat full of bells, lantern striped like a candy cane. He called himself Jingles and muttered about a stash and hoped "the tree" wouldn't find out. I rustled, introduced myself, and he screamed so hard squirrels fell out of their nest.

Between sniffles, Jingles confessed. Santa had "outsourced" to small-town outposts (the North Pole too commercial, he said), and Elmswick's lights weren't just decorations; they were magical conduits. Without them, the sleigh couldn't find its runway, the reindeer wouldn't power up, and presents would go undelivered. Jingles was the watchman who'd somehow lost everything. He feared exile to the Island of Misfit Paperclips. I promised to help. Yes, I'm a tree. No, that doesn't mean I'm useless.

I spread my awareness through frozen soil and snow. Something big and clumsy had dragged the lights deeper into the woods, leaving crushed snow and snapped candy canes. Not fox. Not deer. Not bear. "Krampus?" Jingles whispered. I've met Krampus; chains-and-coal is his brand, not twinkle theft. This felt hungrier.

We found it by moonlight: a Gnarf—part bear, part moth, part goat, all appetite—coiled in a nest of tangled light strings, pressing them against its fur and soaking up their glow like a moth basking in a flame. With every pulse it brightened and hummed. If it drained the magic completely, the lights would be dead glass and Christmas would be done. Jingles begged for action. I don't sprint, but I do launch pinecones. One smacked its nose, another bounced off its belly, a third stuck in its mane. The Gnarf turned toward me, eyes bright as tinsel, and charged. At the last second, Jingles flared his candy-cane lantern, pure Christmas magic. Blinded, the creature tripped into a snowbank.

Up close, I sensed something beneath the fury: loneliness. Jingles explained that long ago Gnarfs were Santa's cleanup crew, eating broken toys, spoiled candy, even defective magic, until one went rogue and the whole species was banished. This one had wandered, starving, and found power in Elmswick's lights. It wasn't trying to ruin Christmas; it wanted a place to belong. We struck a deal: it would untangle the lights and bring them back to town, and we'd give it a role in the festivities—a steady diet of leftover magic instead of stolen glow.

Jingles led the Gnarf back to town while I watched through my roots, feeling every footstep in the frozen earth. The sight of a glowing half-goat monster nearly floored the townsfolk, but the moment the lights flared on, joy drowned fear. Children cheered, the mayor cried, and Santa himself arrived at the forest's edge to thank me personally, laughing at the idea of a tree detective, an overeager elf, and a rehabilitated Gnarf. The townspeople hung the missing star on my highest branch as thanks, and I shone brighter than ever—no moving required.

Now Elmswick celebrates Gnarfmas alongside Christmas. Jingles gives speeches no one requests. And I stand at the forest's edge, watchful and a touch smug, proof that even a pine can save a holiday—and judge your ornament choices—without moving an inch. After all, I am the pine who knew too much.
